title: Paul Revere's Engraving of the Boston Massacre
subject_matter: British soldiers firing on colonists; orderly massacre framing
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Boston Massacre (1770)
themes: Colonial resistance; propaganda; anti-British sentiment; media manipulation
significance: One of the most politically consequential images in American history — Revere's engraving was widely distributed and deliberately falsified to inflame anti-British sentiment; directly fed revolutionary mobilization
notes: Revere copied it from Henry Pelham without credit; its propagandistic distortion is itself historically significant

title: Join or Die
political_context: French and Indian War; colonial unity
subject_matter: Severed snake divided into colonial segments
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Albany Congress (1754); later the American Revolution
themes: Colonial unity; political cartoon as weapon; national identity origins
significance: First American political cartoon; republished during Revolution as call for colonial unity; invented the genre of American political visual rhetoric
notes: Originally for French and Indian War; reused 1765–1776 as revolutionary symbol

title: Washington Crossing the Delaware
subject_matter: Washington; soldiers; ice-filled river; dawn crossing
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Battle of Trenton (1776)
themes: Heroic nationalism; myth-making; leadership; American resilience
significance: Painted during 1848 European revolutions as inspiration for democratic movements; became defining image of American heroic leadership
notes: Painted in Düsseldorf, not America; deliberately mythologized scale and composition

title: The Apotheosis of Washington
subject_matter: Washington ascending to godhood surrounded by allegorical figures
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Civil War end; national reunification
themes: Civil religion; founding mythology; American exceptionalism
significance: Completed the year the Civil War ended; fuses classical mythology with American founding to produce a civic religion

title: Declaration of Independence (painting)
subject_matter: Founding Fathers presenting the Declaration
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Declaration of Independence (1776)
themes: National founding mythology; democratic ideals; selective memory
significance: Commissioned for the Capitol Rotunda; became the dominant visual memory of the founding; notably excludes enslaved people from a document signed by slaveholders
notes: Trumbull worked from life for some portraits; others reconstructed

title: The Death of General Wolfe
subject_matter: Dying British general surrounded by soldiers; Indigenous observer
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Battle of the Plains of Abraham (1759)
themes: Empire; sacrifice; Indigenous witness; war heroism
significance: First major painting to depict contemporary figures in modern dress rather than classical costume; shaped how war death was visualized; Indigenous figure later analyzed as colonial symbol
notes: West was American-born; painting held in Canada; pivotal influence on American history painting

title: Slavery as It Exists in America / Slavery as It Exists in England (abolitionist broadside)
subject_matter: Side-by-side comparison of slavery and English poverty
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Antebellum slavery debate
themes: Abolitionism; counter-propaganda; visual argument; transatlantic debate
significance: Broadside distributed to counter pro-slavery argument that English workers fared worse; shows how visual media shaped abolition debate
notes: Part of a vast genre of antebellum abolitionist visual culture

title: Am I Not a Man and a Brother (Wedgwood medallion)
political_context: British abolitionist movement widely circulated in US
subject_matter: Kneeling enslaved man in chains; supplicant pose
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Transatlantic abolitionist movement
themes: Slavery; humanitarian appeal; paternalism; abolitionist iconography
significance: One of the most widely reproduced anti-slavery images; circulated in US as abolitionist pamphlet illustration; its paternalistic framing later criticized by Black scholars
notes: British origin; central to American abolitionist visual culture

title: The Scourged Back (Gordon / Whipped Peter photograph)
subject_matter: Scarred back of an escaped enslaved man named Gordon
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Civil War; abolitionism; Emancipation Proclamation
themes: Evidence of slavery's brutality; photographic truth; propaganda; Black body as testimony
significance: Published in Harper's Weekly 1863; reached millions; used as recruiting tool for Black Union soldiers; changed Northern public opinion on slavery
notes: Gordon (known as 'Whipped Peter') escaped to Union lines; his name and story were later recovered by historians

title: A Ride for Liberty — The Fugitive Slaves
subject_matter: Black family on horseback fleeing at dawn
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Civil War; Underground Railroad
themes: Black agency; freedom; family; risk
significance: Johnson witnessed a similar escape near Manassas; one of few pre-Emancipation paintings depicting Black self-liberation rather than passive suffering

title: The First Vote
subject_matter: Black men lining up to vote for the first time
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Reconstruction; 15th Amendment (1870)
themes: Black suffrage; Reconstruction promise; citizenship
significance: Published in Harper's Weekly Nov. 1867; documented the revolutionary moment of Black male enfranchisement; widely circulated nationally
source_used: Library of Congress / Harper's Weekly archive

title: Worse than Slavery
political_context: Redemption era; KKK violence; end of Reconstruction
subject_matter: KKK and White League figures clasping hands over Black family huddled in terror
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: End of Reconstruction; KKK violence
themes: Racial terror; Redemption; Reconstruction betrayal; political cartoon as documentation
significance: Nast's cartoons were read by millions; Lincoln called him 'our best recruiting sergeant'; this image directly named the terror dismantling Reconstruction
notes: Nast also invented the visual image of Santa Claus and the Democratic donkey/Republican elephant

title: Boss Tweed cartoon series
subject_matter: Rotund corrupt politician; money bags; Tammany tiger
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Tammany Hall; Boss Tweed's arrest (1873)
themes: Political corruption; press freedom; cartoon as political weapon
significance: Tweed allegedly said 'stop them damn pictures'; Nast's cartoons directly contributed to Tweed's downfall and arrest; established political cartooning as a democratic institution

title: Freedman's Bureau broadside and imagery
subject_matter: Black and white figures; Bureau as mediator
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Freedmen's Bureau (1865–1872)
themes: Reconstruction; federal intervention; Black citizenship; political symbolism
significance: Visual materials surrounding the Bureau documented and shaped public debate about federal responsibility to formerly enslaved people
notes: Genre entry representing Reconstruction-era visual culture broadly

title: The Homestead Strike (illustrated coverage)
subject_matter: Armed Pinkerton agents; striking steel workers; river battle
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Homestead Strike (1892)
themes: Labor rights; corporate power; class warfare; press coverage
significance: Illustrated newspapers brought the Homestead battle into middle-class homes; shaped public sympathy and the labor rights debate
source_used: Library of Congress / Harper's Weekly archive

title: In the Land of the Free
subject_matter: Chinese man separated from family by 'Law' figure; Statue of Liberty in background
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Chinese Exclusion Act (1882); Immigration Act (1917)
themes: Immigration; racial exclusion; irony of American freedom
significance: Published in Chinese-language press; one of the most pointed visual critiques of American immigration racism by an Asian American voice
notes: Ng Poon Chew was a pioneering Chinese American journalist

title: I Want You for U.S. Army (Uncle Sam poster)
subject_matter: Uncle Sam pointing directly at viewer
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: US entry into WWI (1917)
themes: Nationalism; military service; direct address; civic duty
significance: Most reproduced poster in American history (over 4 million copies in WWI alone); recycled for WWII; defined Uncle Sam's visual identity
notes: Flagg used his own face as the model for Uncle Sam

title: Destroy This Mad Brute (WWI recruitment poster)
subject_matter: Gorilla with spiked helmet carrying a woman labeled 'Europe'; clubs labeled 'Kultur'
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: US entry into WWI; anti-German propaganda
themes: Dehumanization; war propaganda; racial/bestial imagery; fear
significance: Exemplifies WWI propaganda's use of racist dehumanization; shaped American public opinion toward entering the war

title: Migrant Mother
subject_matter: Florence Owens Thompson with children; worry; hunger
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Great Depression; New Deal Farm Security Administration
themes: Poverty; motherhood; documentary ethics; government intervention
significance: Commissioned by the FSA to build political support for New Deal relief programs; became the defining image of the Depression; subject Florence Owens Thompson was later identified as Oklahoman of Cherokee descent
notes: Lange never paid Thompson; Thompson later said she wished the photo had never been taken — raises ethics of documentary photography

title: We Can Do It! (Rosie the Riveter)
political_context: WWII homefront; women in wartime labor
subject_matter: Woman in polka-dot bandana flexing bicep
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: WWII homefront mobilization; women entering workforce
themes: Women's labor; wartime gender shift; female strength; later feminist icon
significance: Originally an internal Westinghouse factory poster; rediscovered in 1980s and became feminist icon; its meaning transformed entirely from wartime morale to women's liberation
notes: The 'Rosie' identity attached to this image retroactively; Norman Rockwell's Saturday Evening Post Rosie is a different image

title: Saturday Evening Post Rosie the Riveter
subject_matter: Muscular woman in overalls eating lunch; foot on Mein Kampf
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: WWII homefront; women's labor
themes: Female power; wartime patriotism; working class; anti-fascism
significance: Rockwell's Rosie was more explicitly political than the Miller poster — her foot resting on Hitler's book makes the ideological stakes clear
notes: Now often overshadowed by the Miller poster but considered more artistically complex

title: Executive Order 9066 visual record — Dorothea Lange's Japanese internment photographs
subject_matter: Japanese Americans being evacuated; families with luggage tags; Manzanar
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Executive Order 9066; Japanese American internment (1942)
themes: Civil liberties; racial targeting; government power; documentary witness
significance: The Army confiscated Lange's negatives for the duration of the war because they were too damning; released after WWII as evidence of the policy's human cost
notes: Government censorship of these images is itself part of their political history

title: No Japs Allowed (signage documentation)
subject_matter: Signs barring Japanese Americans from businesses and neighborhoods
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Japanese American internment; racial exclusion
themes: Racial segregation; civil rights; property rights; systemic exclusion
significance: Photographic documentation of exclusion signs became evidence in postwar and redress debates; visualized the texture of institutionalized racism
notes: Documentary evidence category

title: Four Freedoms series (Freedom of Speech)
subject_matter: Working man standing to speak at a town meeting
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: FDR's Four Freedoms speech (1941); WWII ideological framing
themes: Freedom of speech; democratic participation; American ideals; wartime morale
significance: FDR's speech articulated war aims as universal freedoms; Rockwell's paintings became the most widely circulated illustrations of American democratic values in history
notes: The series raised over $132 million in war bonds

title: Four Freedoms series (Freedom from Want)
political_context: WWII; American abundance vs. Axis scarcity
subject_matter: Family gathered at Thanksgiving table; grandmother serving turkey
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: WWII homefront ideology; American prosperity narrative
themes: Abundance; family; American Dream; ideological warfare
significance: Used to contrast American prosperity with Axis-dominated Europe; later critiqued for its exclusion of non-white families from its vision of 'American' abundance

title: The Problem We All Live With
subject_matter: Ruby Bridges escorted by US Marshals; racial slur on wall
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: New Orleans school desegregation (1960); Civil Rights Act (1964)
themes: Civil rights; childhood innocence; state protection; racial hatred
significance: Published in Look magazine; Robert F. Kennedy hung it outside the Oval Office during a meeting with civil rights leaders; one of the most politically resonant American paintings of the 20th century
notes: Rockwell left the Saturday Evening Post specifically to paint civil rights subjects they refused to publish

title: The Murder of Emmett Till (documentation and press photography)
subject_matter: Open casket showing Till's brutalized face
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Murder of Emmett Till (1955); Civil Rights mobilization
themes: Lynching; racial terror; mother's choice; media as witness
significance: Mamie Till insisted on an open casket and allowed Jet Magazine to publish the photographs; she said she wanted the world to see what they did to her son; the images directly catalyzed the Civil Rights Movement
notes: The decision to publish is one of the most consequential editorial choices in American media history

title: I Have a Dream (documentary photography)
political_context: Civil Rights Movement; March on Washington
subject_matter: MLK at podium; crowd at the National Mall
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: March on Washington (1963)
themes: Civil rights; oratory; mass movement; visual documentation
significance: The photographs of the March on Washington — the crowd's scale, the reflecting pool, King at the podium — created the enduring visual record of the movement's peak moment
notes: Photographic documentation as political artifact

title: Black Power salute (1968 Olympics photograph)
subject_matter: Tommie Smith and John Carlos raising fists on podium; Peter Norman in solidarity
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Mexico City Olympics (1968); Black Power Movement
themes: Protest; Black Power; international solidarity; athletic platform
significance: One of the most reproduced protest photographs in history; Smith and Carlos were suspended from the team; the image became the defining symbol of athlete political protest
notes: Peter Norman (the Australian) wore an Olympic Project for Human Rights badge in solidarity; he was ostracized in Australia

title: Napalm Girl (The Terror of War)
subject_matter: Naked nine-year-old Kim Phuc running from napalm attack
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Vietnam War; public opposition to US military intervention
themes: War's cost; civilian suffering; anti-war sentiment; press freedom
significance: Won the Pulitzer Prize; credited with turning American public opinion against the Vietnam War; Nixon initially questioned whether it was staged
notes: Kim Phuc survived; she and Ut have maintained a relationship for decades; the photo's publication over nudity objections was a landmark press freedom moment

title: Execution of a Viet Cong Prisoner
subject_matter: General Loan shooting prisoner in the head at point blank range
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Tet Offensive (1968); Vietnam War public opinion shift
themes: Execution; war's reality; shock; moral ambiguity
significance: Won the Pulitzer Prize; helped turn American public opinion on the war; Adams later said the photo destroyed Loan's life and expressed regret — raises questions of photographic context
notes: Adams said: 'The general killed the Viet Cong; I killed the general with my camera'

title: Vietnam Veterans Memorial
subject_matter: Reflective black wall with 58
themes: Vietnam War; veterans' commemoration
significance: Memory; grief; democratic naming; healing vs. heroism
source_used: Lin's design was controversial — veterans' groups called it a 'black gash of shame'; it became the most visited memorial in Washington and transformed memorial design worldwide
notes: National Mall records
column18: Lin was 21 and a Yale architecture student when she won the competition; her race and gender were weaponized against her design

title: AIDS Quilt (NAMES Project Memorial Quilt)
subject_matter: Panels for each AIDS victim; personal names; personal objects
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: AIDS crisis (1980s–1990s); LGBTQ+ rights movement
themes: Grief; LGBTQ+ visibility; political neglect; collective mourning
significance: First displayed on the National Mall in 1987 with 1,920 panels; by 1992 it covered the entire Mall; made AIDS victims visible to a government that had ignored the epidemic for years
notes: Now has over 50,000 panels; each one is a political act of naming the dead the government refused to count

title: Silence = Death (ACT UP poster)
subject_matter: Pink triangle on black; text: SILENCE = DEATH
themes: AIDS crisis; ACT UP direct action
significance: LGBTQ+ rights; government inaction; political urgency; visual activism
source_used: The pink triangle reclaimed a Nazi symbol (gay men in concentration camps) and redeployed it as a call to activism; became the visual language of AIDS activism globally
notes: ACT UP archive
column18: Designed by six gay men; the Silence = Death Project predated ACT UP; the poster was later adopted by ACT UP

title: Guerrilla Girls posters (Do Women Have to Be Naked?)
subject_matter: Nude woman with gorilla head; Metropolitan Museum statistics
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Feminist art movement; museum gatekeeping
themes: Gender inequality in art institutions; humor as protest; anonymity; institutional critique
significance: The Guerrilla Girls used billboard and poster tactics to expose the Met's collection demographics (5% women artists, 85% female nudes); transformed how institutions discussed representation
notes: Collective used gorilla masks and dead female artists' names as pseudonyms to keep focus on statistics not personalities

title: The Liberation of Aunt Jemima
political_context: Black Arts Movement; Black Power; feminist art
subject_matter: Aunt Jemima figure holding rifle and grenade; raised fist
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Black Power Movement; Black Arts Movement
themes: Stereotype reclamation; Black Power; feminist critique; consumer racism
significance: Saar transforms a racist advertising icon into a symbol of Black female resistance; landmark of Black feminist assemblage art; made during the most intense phase of Black Power

title: I Am a Man (sanitation workers' strike photograph)
subject_matter: Black sanitation workers in Memphis holding 'I Am a Man' signs
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Memphis sanitation strike (1968); MLK assassination
themes: Labor rights; racial dignity; civil rights; King's final campaign
significance: King was assassinated in Memphis supporting this strike; the 'I Am a Man' signs directly challenged the dehumanization of Black workers; photograph became icon of labor-civil rights intersection
notes: Withers was later revealed to have also been an FBI informant — a profound complication to his legacy as a civil rights photographer

title: Bloody Sunday photographs (Selma)
political_context: Civil Rights Movement; Voting Rights Act
subject_matter: State troopers attacking peaceful marchers on Edmund Pettus Bridge
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Bloody Sunday (March 7 1965); Voting Rights Act (1965)
themes: Voting rights; state violence; nonviolent resistance; media witness
significance: ABC interrupted Judgment at Nuremberg to broadcast footage; the juxtaposition of Nazi atrocity and American state violence shocked the nation; LBJ introduced the Voting Rights Act 8 days later
notes: The visual parallel of Nazi and American state violence was unplanned but devastating

title: Wounded Knee occupation photographs
subject_matter: AIM members at Wounded Knee; armed standoff with federal marshals
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Wounded Knee occupation (1973); AIM; Indigenous rights movement
themes: Indigenous sovereignty; treaty rights; occupation; media strategy
significance: AIM chose Wounded Knee (site of the 1890 massacre) deliberately; the 71-day occupation and its photographs forced national conversation on treaty rights and Indigenous sovereignty
notes: Photography as media strategy by AIM; they understood the power of the site's symbolism

title: Times Up and MeToo visual movement (Tarana Burke / protest imagery)
medium: Mixed media (posters; social media; protest art)
subject_matter: Women in black at Golden Globes; protest signs; survivor portraits
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: MeToo movement (2017); Times Up
themes: Women's rights; sexual violence; accountability; collective action
significance: The visual culture of MeToo — black dresses at award shows, protest signs, social media graphics — constitutes a political art movement using image to shift cultural power
notes: Tarana Burke founded MeToo in 2006; the movement's 2017 viral moment was amplified by visual solidarity actions

title: Hope (Obama campaign poster)
subject_matter: Barack Obama in red/blue/beige; word HOPE below
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: 2008 presidential election; Barack Obama campaign
themes: Electoral politics; visual identity; grassroots art; image as campaign tool
significance: Became the defining image of the 2008 Obama campaign and one of the most reproduced political artworks in American history; later entangled in copyright controversy over the AP source photograph
notes: Fairey settled with the AP over the source photograph; the case became landmark in copyright and political art

title: Portrait of Barack Obama
subject_matter: Obama seated amid flowering plants
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Obama presidency; representation in American institutions
themes: Black representation; presidential canon; art historical revisionism
significance: First presidential portrait by a Black artist in the National Portrait Gallery; transformed a 200-year tradition of white male official portraiture; the floral background references power and non-Western aesthetic traditions

title: Portrait of Michelle Obama
political_context: First Black First Lady; official portraiture
subject_matter: Michelle Obama in billowing pale blue dress
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Obama presidency; representation in American institutions
themes: Black womanhood; dignity; portraiture history; grisaille skin
significance: First Black woman to paint an official White House portrait; Sherald's use of grisaille (gray) skin tones makes Blackness a category rather than a color — a deliberate philosophical statement

title: Untitled (I Can't Breathe)
subject_matter: Text; George Floyd; Eric Garner; protest imagery
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Deaths of Eric Garner (2014) and George Floyd (2020); BLM movement
themes: Police violence; racial justice; mourning; protest art
significance: The phrase 'I Can't Breathe' became a visual and political refrain across murals, prints, and protest signs nationally; BLM visual culture constitutes one of the largest grassroots art movements in American history
notes: Umbrella entry for BLM visual culture; specific works could be disaggregated

title: George Floyd Memorial Mural (Minneapolis)
artist: Xena Goldman / Cadex Herrera / Greta McLain
subject_matter: George Floyd portrait; flowers; 'I Can't Breathe'
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Murder of George Floyd (2020); BLM global protests
themes: Mourning; public space; community memorial; protest
significance: The mural at the site of Floyd's murder became a global pilgrimage site; its imagery was reproduced on every continent; demonstrates how street art became the primary visual language of contemporary political protest
notes: Site became known as George Floyd Square; the mural has been repainted and maintained by the community

title: The Dinner Party
subject_matter: Triangular table; 39 place settings for historical women; 999 names on floor
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Second-wave feminism; women's history movement
themes: Women's history; craft as fine art; feminist canon-making; sexual iconography
significance: The most politically programmatic major artwork of second-wave feminism; forced art institutions to grapple with what 'women's history' means and who gets commemorated; rejected by multiple museums before Brooklyn
notes: The explicit vulvar imagery on place settings caused significant controversy; several universities refused to house it

title: Your Body Is a Battleground
political_context: Abortion rights; Roe v. Wade context
subject_matter: Split positive/negative female face; text overlay
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: 1989 reproductive rights march on Washington; Roe v. Wade
themes: Reproductive rights; female body as political site; advertising aesthetics
significance: Created specifically for the April 1989 march in Washington for reproductive rights; its advertising aesthetic made it reproducible across protest contexts; text treats the female body as contested political territory

title: Chicano Park Murals (San Diego)
subject_matter: Aztec imagery; labor history; Cesar Chavez; Brown Berets; community history
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Chicano movement (1969–1970s); community organizing against urban renewal
themes: Chicano identity; labor rights; Indigenous heritage; community resistance
significance: Created after activists occupied land slated for a highway patrol parking lot; the murals transformed freeway infrastructure into the most important outdoor Chicano art site in the US
source_used: City of San Diego / Chicano Park Steering Committee records
notes: National Historic Landmark since 2016; still being painted

title: Migrant farmworker photographs (Cesar Chavez / UFW)
political_context: United Farm Workers; labor rights; Chicano movement
subject_matter: Farmworkers; picket lines; Cesar Chavez; grape boycott
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: United Farm Workers strikes (1965–1970); grape and lettuce boycotts
themes: Labor rights; immigration; dignity; nonviolent protest
significance: The visual documentation of UFW campaigns — picket lines, the black eagle flag, hunger strikes — built national support for the boycott; photographs put faces on the hidden labor of food production

title: Piss Christ
political_context: Culture wars; First Amendment; NEA funding debate
subject_matter: Crucifix submerged in artist's urine; amber glow
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: NEA culture wars (1989–1990); First Amendment debate
themes: Religious freedom; government arts funding; censorship; sacred vs. profane
significance: Senator Jesse Helms used the work to argue against NEA funding; it became the flashpoint of the 1980s–90s culture wars over government arts funding and free expression; the work itself barely changed — the political controversy is the history
notes: The photograph was physically attacked in France in 2011 by Christian fundamentalists

title: The Two Undiscovered Amerindians
political_context: Columbus quincentenary; Indigenous rights; colonial gaze
subject_matter: Two artists in cage, presented as 'undiscovered' Indigenous people from a fictional island
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Columbus quincentenary (1992); Indigenous rights discourse
themes: Colonial gaze; museum complicity; performative critique; Indigenous dehumanization
significance: Performed in museums worldwide; many audience members believed the performers were real; the performance exposed audiences' willingness to accept the dehumanizing display of non-white bodies as natural
notes: The discomfort of audiences who didn't realize it was performance is itself the political data

title: We Are All Suspects (post-9/11 visual culture)
political_context: Post-9/11 surveillance; Patriot Act; racial profiling
subject_matter: Surveillance cameras; Muslim figures; airport security imagery
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: September 11 attacks (2001); Patriot Act; War on Terror
themes: Civil liberties; Islamophobia; surveillance state; racial profiling
significance: Post-9/11 visual culture — posters, murals, street art, protest graphics — documented and contested the rapid expansion of surveillance and the targeting of Muslim Americans
notes: Umbrella entry for post-9/11 political visual culture

title: Homeland Security Blanket
political_context: Indigenous rights; border security; settler colonialism
subject_matter: Thermal emergency blanket design woven in Tlingit style with 'HOMELAND SECURITY' text
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: US immigration detention; Indigenous sovereignty; border politics
themes: Settler colonialism; immigration; Indigenous land; historical irony
significance: Galanin weaves the mylar emergency blankets used to wrap detained migrants (including Indigenous migrants) in traditional Tlingit weaving patterns; the phrase 'Homeland Security' applied to Indigenous art inverts its entire meaning
notes: One of the most conceptually precise political artworks of the contemporary period

title: Ghost Army visual deception program
medium: Inflatable tanks; sound deception; visual camouflage
political_context: WWII military strategy; art in service of war
institution: National Archives / Ghost Army Legacy Project
subject_matter: Inflatable Sherman tanks; fake unit insignia; deceptive radio traffic
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: WWII European theater; Operation Fortitude
themes: Art and war; deception; visual illusion; classified history
significance: The Ghost Army was largely composed of artists, designers, and creative professionals who used visual deception to fool the German army; classified for 40+ years; extraordinary story of artists as military strategists
source_used: National Archives / Ghost Army Legacy Project
notes: Congress awarded the Ghost Army the Congressional Gold Medal in 2022

title: The Trail of Tears (painting series)
subject_matter: Thousands of Cherokee marching; soldiers; winter
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Indian Removal Act (1830); Trail of Tears
themes: Indigenous displacement; genocide; forced march
significance: Painted a century after the event; became the standard visual reference for the Trail of Tears in textbooks; its retrospective nature raises questions of who gets to visualize others' trauma
notes: The most widely reproduced Trail of Tears image despite being painted 112 years after the event by a white artist

title: Totem poles and Indigenous visual sovereignty (documentation)
artist: Various Indigenous artists; documented by Edward Curtis and others
political_context: Indigenous cultural survival; cultural erasure; sovereignty
subject_matter: Totem poles; ceremonial objects; community gatherings
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Potlatch Ban (1885–1951 Canada); American Indian Religious Freedom Act (1978)
themes: Cultural survival; sovereignty; artistic resistance to assimilation
significance: The banning of potlatches and ceremonial art was a direct political act; the survival and revival of Northwest Coast art is a political story of resistance; Curtis's documentary photography simultaneously preserved and aestheticized Indigenous culture
notes: The Potlatch Ban is a Canadian policy but its logic directly informed US Indian boarding school cultural suppression

title: The Black Panther newspaper illustrations
subject_matter: Armed Black figures; pigs as police; community food programs; revolutionary imagery
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Black Panther Party (1966–1982); Black Power movement
themes: Black Power; revolutionary iconography; community organizing; press as weapon
significance: Douglas served as Minister of Culture for the Black Panthers; his illustrations in the Party newspaper reached hundreds of thousands weekly; he invented the visual language of Black Power
notes: The newspaper had a circulation of ~100,000 at its peak; Douglas influenced generations of political graphic artists globally

title: Chicano moratorium photographs
political_context: Vietnam War; Chicano antiwar movement; police violence
subject_matter: Chicano antiwar demonstrators; police tear gas; Ruben Salazar
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Chicano Moratorium (August 29 1970); Ruben Salazar killing
themes: Vietnam War opposition; Chicano rights; police violence; press freedom
significance: The largest Chicano demonstration in US history; reporter Ruben Salazar was killed by sheriff's deputies; photographs documented both the movement and the state violence that ended it
notes: Salazar was a prominent journalist; his killing by police during the demonstration shaped Chicano political consciousness

title: Raising the Flag on Iwo Jima
subject_matter: Six Marines raising flag on Mount Suribachi
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Battle of Iwo Jima (1945); WWII Pacific theater
themes: National heroism; sacrifice; collective effort; war morale
significance: Won the Pulitzer Prize the same year it was taken (the only photo to do so); used immediately for the 7th War Loan Drive; became the basis for the Marine Corps War Memorial; one of the most reproduced photographs in history
notes: Three of the six flag-raisers died in subsequent Iwo Jima fighting; one Marine was misidentified for decades

title: Dorothea Lange's Japanese internment photographs (confiscated series)
political_context: Japanese American internment; government suppression of evidence
subject_matter: Families lined up for forced removal; Manzanar life; watch towers
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Executive Order 9066 (1942); Japanese American incarceration
themes: Civil liberties; racial targeting; state suppression of truth; documentary ethics
significance: The Army confiscated Lange's photographs because they were too sympathetic to the internees; they were released after the war and became key evidence in the redress movement and eventual government apology
notes: Separate entry from Item 22 focusing on the censorship/suppression dimension

title: With Liberty and Justice for All (suffrage and 19th Amendment visual culture)
subject_matter: Women at polling places; anti-suffrage cartoons; suffrage parades
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: 19th Amendment (1920); Women's suffrage
themes: Women's rights; voting; gender and citizenship; visual debate
significance: The visual culture of the suffrage movement — banners, posters, political cartoons both for and against — constitutes a 70-year argument about democracy conducted through images
notes: Complementary to Jasmitha's women's suffrage dataset; focuses on visual artifacts

title: Anti-Vietnam War protest posters (Peace movement visual culture)
subject_matter: Doves; napalm imagery; body counts; draft cards
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Vietnam War antiwar movement; Kent State (1970)
themes: Antiwar activism; peace; draft resistance; visual counterculture
significance: The antiwar movement produced some of the most innovative political graphic design in American history; Corita Kent's use of commercial aesthetics for antiwar messages anticipated later political art strategies
notes: Corita Kent (Sister Corita) was a Catholic nun and art teacher whose work reached millions

title: Kent State photograph (May 4 1970)
political_context: Vietnam War; Kent State massacre; student protest
subject_matter: Mary Ann Vecchio kneeling over Jeffrey Miller's body; anguish
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Kent State massacre (May 4 1970); Cambodia invasion protests
themes: Student protest; state violence; antiwar movement; grief
significance: Won the Pulitzer Prize; appeared on front pages worldwide the next day; gave Crosby Stills Nash & Young their song 'Ohio'; directly increased antiwar protests nationally
notes: Vecchio was a 14-year-old runaway; she was later hounded and harassed in her home state of Florida

title: The 1619 Project cover art
subject_matter: Slave ship detail on cover; 1619 masthead
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: 1619 Project publication (2019); debates over slavery in American founding
themes: Historical memory; slavery; American founding; curriculum politics
significance: The visual presentation of the 1619 Project — cover art, photo essays, layout — made a historiographical argument about America's true founding date; triggered national curriculum debates and a Pulitzer Prize
notes: The project's visual choices were as deliberate as its text; the cover image (a detail of a slave ship) made a political argument before the reader opened the magazine

title: March on Washington photographs (1963)
artist: various (Bob Adelman; Charles Moore; Flip Schulke)
political_context: Civil Rights Movement; March on Washington
subject_matter: Crowd of 250,000; Lincoln Memorial; MLK at podium; integrated masses
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom (August 28 1963)
themes: Civil rights; mass protest; integration; the Mall as political space
significance: The photographs of the March established the National Mall as the stage for American democratic protest; the scale of the crowd — photographed from the Washington Monument — became proof of movement strength
notes: The integration visible in the crowd was itself a political statement; photographers documented both the speakers and the sea of people

title: Ferguson protest murals and protest art
medium: Street murals; protest signs; social media
political_context: Ferguson uprising; Black Lives Matter founding
subject_matter: Michael Brown; hands up don't shoot; community grief and rage
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Murder of Michael Brown (2014); Ferguson uprising; BLM founding
themes: Police violence; Black Lives Matter; community art; protest as memorial
significance: The 'Hands Up Don't Shoot' gesture became a visual language before it became a slogan; Ferguson protest art founded the visual vocabulary of the BLM movement
notes: The gesture spread globally; its visual simplicity made it infinitely reproducible

title: Black Lives Matter street mural (Washington DC)
artist: Various artists commissioned by DC Mayor Muriel Bowser
political_context: George Floyd protests; BLM; government response to protest
subject_matter: Giant yellow 'BLACK LIVES MATTER' letters on street leading to White House
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: George Floyd protests (2020); BLM movement
themes: Public space; political statement; government co-optation; visual power
significance: DC Mayor had the mural painted on the street leading to the White House; BLM organizers added 'Defund the Police' nearby; the tension between official and activist versions of the same space is itself a political argument
notes: The mural was later extended and made permanent; contested as co-optation vs. endorsement by different BLM factions

title: Equal Justice Initiative lynching memorial (documentation)
political_context: Racial terror; lynching history; restorative justice
institution: National Memorial for Peace and Justice Montgomery AL
subject_matter: Steel monuments for each county where lynchings occurred; hanging steel; names engraved
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: History of racial terror lynching in America; contemporary racial justice
themes: Memory; racial terror; public accounting; memorial design
significance: The National Memorial is the first major American memorial to victims of racial terror lynching; its design — steel monuments that evoke hanging bodies — was a political and architectural choice that refuses comfort
notes: The EJI left duplicate monuments for counties to claim and install locally; most counties have not claimed them — itself a political fact

title: We Shall Overcome (visual documentation of the song and its use)
subject_matter: Demonstrators linking arms and singing; marches; funerals
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Civil Rights Movement broadly; Selma; Birmingham
themes: Song as visual-political symbol; solidarity; nonviolent protest
significance: The image of hands linked while singing 'We Shall Overcome' became a universal visual symbol of nonviolent resistance; photographs of it at marches, funerals, and confrontations constituted a visual theology of the movement
notes: The song itself has a complex history from gospel to labor movement to civil rights

title: The Farm Security Administration photo archive (as a whole)
artist: Dorothea Lange; Walker Evans; Gordon Parks; others
political_context: New Deal; Great Depression documentation; poverty politics
subject_matter: Rural poverty; sharecroppers; Dust Bowl; migrant camps; Black poverty
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: New Deal (1933–1938); Great Depression politics
themes: Government photography as political instrument; poverty visibility; documentary ethics
significance: The FSA archive was created to build political support for New Deal programs; it is simultaneously the greatest documentary photography project in American history and an example of government-directed image production
notes: Gordon Parks joined the FSA and created foundational images of Black American life; his work expands the archive beyond its initial white rural focus

title: American Progress
subject_matter: Angelic Columbia figure floating west; telegraph wire; settlers following; Native peoples and animals fleeing before her
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Manifest Destiny ideology; westward expansion; Indian Removal
themes: Manifest Destiny; settler colonialism; racial hierarchy; progress ideology
significance: Widely reproduced as chromolithograph; became the defining visual statement of Manifest Destiny ideology; the fleeing Native Americans and animals in the 'darkness' of the west made white supremacist expansion look divinely ordained
notes: Commissioned by George Crofutt for his travel guide; instantly mass-reproduced; shaped how millions of Americans understood westward expansion

title: Bury My Heart at Wounded Knee (documentary photographs of 1890 massacre aftermath)
subject_matter: Mass grave; frozen Lakota bodies; Big Foot among the dead
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Wounded Knee Massacre (December 29 1890)
themes: Indigenous genocide; US military violence; end of Indian Wars
significance: The Army's own photographs of the mass grave became evidence of the massacre; they were suppressed for decades but when published in the 1970s they fueled AIM and the American Indian Movement's political arguments
notes: Some soldiers received the Medal of Honor for Wounded Knee; a campaign to rescind these medals has been ongoing

title: The Jungle (visual adaptations and illustrations)
artist: Various illustrators for Upton Sinclair's novel
political_context: Progressive Era; meatpacking industry; labor and immigration
subject_matter: Meatpacking workers; contaminated food; immigrant labor
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Pure Food and Drug Act (1906); meatpacking regulation; Progressive Era
themes: Labor exploitation; immigration; food safety; corporate power
significance: Sinclair wrote The Jungle to expose the exploitation of immigrant workers; readers responded to the food contamination descriptions instead; visual adaptations amplified both messages; the book directly led to the Pure Food and Drug Act
notes: Sinclair said: 'I aimed at the public's heart and by accident hit it in the stomach'

title: Fallen Astronauts memorial and Space Race visual culture
political_context: Space Race; Cold War; NASA program
subject_matter: Buzz Aldrin on the moon; earthrise photograph; Apollo 1 memorial
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: Apollo 11 moon landing (1969); Apollo 1 fire (1967); Cold War
themes: American achievement; Cold War competition; technological nationalism; sacrifice
significance: The Earthrise photograph (William Anders 1968) and the moon landing images became the most politically potent symbols of American Cold War supremacy; they also produced a new vision of Earth as fragile and unified
notes: Earthrise is sometimes credited with inspiring the first Earth Day (1970) and the environmental movement

title: Untitled (Perfect Lovers)
political_context: AIDS crisis; LGBTQ+ love and loss; Reagan-era neglect
subject_matter: Two identical round clocks side by side; synchronized; slowly falling out of sync
historical_event_depicted_or_influenced: AIDS crisis; LGBTQ+ rights; Reagan administration's silence
themes: Love; loss; time; political neglect; grief
significance: Made as Gonzalez-Torres's partner Ross died of AIDS; the clocks slowly fall out of sync as their batteries die at different rates; the piece is simultaneously a love poem and an indictment of the government that let Ross die; Gonzalez-Torres himself died of AIDS in 1996
notes: One of the most politically devastating artworks of the late 20th century in its restraint; the politics are entirely in what the viewer knows about its context
